issue.title: binaries in yasa-linux-x64.zip have no execution permission

I downloaded [yasa-linux-x64.zip](https://github.com/antgroup/YASA-Engine/releases/download/v0.2.30/yasa-linux-x64.zip) but found that binaries did not have execution permission.

```bash
$ zipinfo yasa-linux-x64.zip
-rw-r--r--  3.0 unx  4324099 b- defN 25-Nov-28 05:25 uast4go-linux-amd64
-rw-r--r--  3.0 unx 24817408 b- defN 25-Nov-28 05:25 uast4py-linux-amd64
-rw-r--r--  3.0 unx 273963615 b- defN 25-Nov-28 05:25 yasa-engine-linux-x64
```

Execution permission might get loss after [uploading artifact](https://github.com/antgroup/YASA-Engine/blob/44269b02dd77869008df2e13b26ddbf984d2454c/.github/workflows/release.yml#L353-L360) as [Permission Loss](https://github.com/actions/upload-artifact?tab=readme-ov-file#permission-loss) mentions if I understand correctly.

issue.title: Query parallelization using `codeql database run-queries`
issue.body: `codeql database run-queries` supports run multiple queries in same database parallelly. Currently `codeql query run` is used but the command will lock the database for each query. The query parallelism provided by codeql itself can be leveraged for faster query speed.

issue.title: runtime: mallocgc does not seem to need to call publicationBarrier when allocating noscan objects

labels.name: NeedsInvestigation
labels.description: Someone must examine and confirm this is a valid issue and not a duplicate of an existing one.

labels.name: compiler/runtime
labels.description: Issues related to the Go compiler and/or runtime.

issue.body:
I'm doing some performance analysis of `go1.20` on `arm64` platform, and I found that the DMB instruction in mallocgc consumes a lot of time. 

I found some CLs：

- [CL11083](https://go-review.googlesource.com/c/go/+/11083):  In `go1.5`, `publicationBarrier` was added when allocating scan objects. 
- [CL23043](https://go-review.googlesource.com/c/go/+/23043):  In `go1.7`, `publicationBarrier` is was added when allocating noscan objects because of the `heapBitsSetTypeNoScan`. 
- [CL41253](https://go-review.googlesource.com/c/go/+/41253):  However, `publicationBarrier` is still called when allocating  noscan objects after `heapBitsSetTypeNoScan` is deleted.

I tried to find the reason why `publicationBarrier` was called when allocating noscan objects, but I couldn't find it.


Knowing from the comments of source code, `publicationBarrier` in `mallocgc` ensure that the stores above that initialize x to type-safe memory and set the heap bits occur before the caller can make x observable to the GC. `publicationBarrier` in `allocSpan` make sure the newly allocated span will be observed by the GC before pointers into the span are published.


My question is, GC don't scan noscan object, why is `publicationBarrier` required when allocating noscan objects in `mallocgc`?

Thank you in advance for your help.

comment.body: I do an experiment to insert nop instructions, which can be replaced by calculation instructions like add and sub before `publicationBarrier` and I find the latency can be masked. Then I move the calculation logic of profiling condition and load instruction of `writeBarrier.enabled` before `publicationBarrier`, but I don't see significant time decreasing for the next instruction of `publicationBarrier`. --- ## Related Work According to [No Barrier in the Road: A Comprehensive Study and Optimization of ARM Barriers](https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3332466.3374535), the latency of the barrier instruction can be masked by `nop` instruction. The conclusion can be infered from the fact that in Algorithm 1 and Figure 3(a) with 150 nops, the throughput of program with DMB full-1, which **is followed by** 150 nop instructions, is half of the throught of program with DMB full-2, which **follows** 150 nop instructions. But Figure 3(a) does not show difference of throughput for DMB st, which is `publicationBarrier` here, between DMB st-1 and DMB st-2. ## Latency Mask by NOP The machine I use is aarch64 server with 64 cores, 2 NUMA nodes, 4MiB L1d Cache, 4MiB L1i Cache, 32MiB L2 Cache, 64MiB L3 Cache. The go version I use is 1.24.2. I add `noploop` function in src/runtime/asm_arm64.s and in stub.go, which simulate the nop loop with no memory accesses. I also add `nop` function to avoid pprof accumulate time to the next instruction of `publicationBarrier`, i.e. `span.freeIndexForScan = span.freeindex`. I modify `mallogcSmallNoscan` and `mallocgcSmallScanNoHeader` to evaluate the performance. ``` TEXT ·noploop(SB),NOSPLIT,$0-8 Loop: CMP $0, R0 BLE End NOP SUB $1, R0 B Loop End: RET TEXT ·nop(SB),NOSPLIT|NOFRAME,$0-0 RET ``` There are 3 configurations: | abbreviation | config | expectation for the config | statements following `publicationBarrier` | profile file | | ------------ | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------- | | nops_mask | `noploop` + `publicationBarrier` + `nop` + `span.freeIndexForScan = span.freeindex` | The latency for `publicationBarrier` should be masked by `noploop` and the time for `nop`+`span.freeIndexForScan = span.freeindex` should be less. | `nop`+`span.freeIndexForScan = span.freeindex` | nops_mask.out | | nodmb | `span.freeIndexForScan = span.freeindex` | The time for `span.freeIndexForScan = span.freeindex` should be less. | `span.freeIndexForScan = span.freeindex`(no DMB ST) | nodmb.out | | default | `publicationBarrier` + `span.freeIndexForScan = span.freeindex` | The time of `span.freeIndexForScan = span.freeindex` is large and can't be ignored in the view of `mallogcSmallNoscan` | `span.freeIndexForScan = span.freeindex` | cpu.out | I run `go test -a -tags=$tag . -bench='^BenchmarkMalloc.*$' -run='none' -cpuprofile $tag.out` in `runtime` in `src/runtime` directory for these 3 configurations. The cpu profiles are in [cpuprofile.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/24364414/cpuprofile.zip) with `pprof.sh` to start pprof web ui and `eager_calc.out` which is mentioned later. ## evaluation results Cummulative time for each config. For a config which does not have code in column1, "-" is used to fill the cell. 0 is used to fill the table if pprof does not show time for the code. mallocgcSmallNoscan | cum | nops_mask | nodmb | default | | ------------------------------------------- | --------- | ----- | ------- | | mallocgcSmallNoscan | 1.18s | 990ms | 1.22s | | noploop(120) | 690ms | - | - | | publicationBarrier | 30ms | - | 20ms | | nop | 190ms | - | - | | span.freeIndexForScan = span.freeindex | 20ms | 50ms | 500ms | | writeBarrier.enabled | 0 | 0 | 0 | | gcmarknewobject | 0 | 10ms | 10ms | | c.nextSample -= int64(size) if c.nextSample < 0 ... | 10ms | 20ms | 10ms | | profilealloc | 0 | 0 | 20ms | mallocgcSmallScanNoHeader | cum | nops_mask | nodmb | default | | ------------------------------------------- | --------- | ----- | ------- | | mallocgcSmallScanNoHeader | 2.47s | 1.99s | 2.29s | | noploop(140) | 1.62s | - | - | | publicationBarrier | 0 | - | 30ms | | nop | 200ms | - | - | | span.freeIndexForScan = span.freeindex | 20ms | 10ms | 820ms | | writeBarrier.enabled | 0 | 0 | 0 | | gcmarknewobject | 10ms | 50ms | 30ms | | c.nextSample -= int64(size) if c.nextSample < 0 ... | 70ms | 50ms | 0 | | profilealloc | 0 | 0 | 20ms | It seems that the latency of `publicationBarrier` and `span.freeIndexForScan = span.freeindex` is masked by `noploop` in nops_mask config compared with the default config. Although `writeBarrier.enabled` load instruction and `c.nextSample -= int64(size) if c.nextSample < 0 ...` calculation expressions do not occupy large part, they can be calculated before `publicationBarrier` eagerly to mask the latency as `noploop` does. So I did another experiment. ## Moving Forward Calculation and Load The forwarded code is as follows. The config is named as "eager_calc". The cpu profile file is "eager_calc.out". ```go wbEnabled := writeBarrier.enabled // eager calc c.nextSample -= int64(size) // eager calc doSample := c.nextSample < 0 || MemProfileRate != c.memProfRate // eager calc publicationBarrier() span.freeIndexForScan = span.freeindex if wbEnabled { gcmarknewobject(span, uintptr(x)) } if doSample { profilealloc(mp, x, size) } mp.mallocing = 0 ``` The comparison of time spent in statements between "eager_calc" and "default" is as follows: In `mallocgcSmallScanNoHeader`, there is not so much difference in the time spent on `publicationBarrier` and `span.freeIndexForScan = span.freeindex`. | cum | eager_calc | default | | ----------------------------------------- | ---------- | ------- | | mallocgcSmallScanNoHeader | 2.36s | 2.21s | | writeBarrier.enabled | 0 | 0 | | c.nextSample -= ..., c.nextSample < 0 ... | 10ms | 40ms | | publicationBarrier | 20ms | 30ms | | span.freeIndexForScan = span.freeindex | 890ms | 850ms | | gcmarknewobject | 0 | 40ms | | profilealloc | 0 | 10ms | In `mallocgcSmallNoscan`, time spent on `publicationBarrier` and `span.freeIndexForScan = span.freeindex` is fewer. | cum | eager_calc | default | | ----------------------------------------- | ---------- | ------- | | mallocgcSmallNoscan | 1.09s | 1.23s | | writeBarrier.enabled | 0 | 0 | | c.nextSample -= ..., c.nextSample < 0 ... | 10ms | 40ms | | publicationBarrier | 0 | 30ms | | span.freeIndexForScan = span.freeindex | 450ms | 570ms | | gcmarknewobject | 0 | 10ms | | profilealloc | 10ms | 10ms | It seems that moving forward calculation of profiling condition and `writeBarrier.enabled` load instruction does not improve the performance stably. Time spent on `writeBarrier.enabled` is always 0. More experiments are required I think. --- Some misgivings: since GC exists, more allocation means more marking jobs to do and the bottleneck for most programs can still be GC marking. **So I'm not sure whether optimization of such subtle mechanism can bring some benefits. The selection of benchmarks is subtle.** Thanks for your reading.

discussion.title: how to use `--external` option of `codeql query run`
discussion.body:
The idea is that I want to add some addition data to codeql database and use these new data as new predicates to enhance the analysis capabilities. I notice the `--external` option, but I don't know how to use it. So I'm asking here.

Idea: The data can be generated by compiler or other static analysis tools. And they are convertible to CodeQL classes/predicates through user-defined mapping rules. For example, for AST nodes, the location string can be converted to AST node if user-defined adaptor predicates are satisfied. Additional information can be inserted to the added data to avoid conflicts when it is generated by compiler or other static analysis tools.

Take escape analysis in golang as an example. Some variables can be heap allocated decided by compiler. We can dump the definition location, the type and other information about these variables to csv file. When imported by CodeQL, we can define a adaptor using the location string and the type to map csv file to `HeapAllocatedVariable` class, then we can do more things through CodeQL, like linters or statistical analysis. It's somewhat a problem about fusing the data generated by another extractor.

Issue https://github.com/github/codeql/issues/9758 also reveals similiar problem I think.

summary_topics.work: The user's primary technical interests revolve around static program analysis, particularly in building and advancing tools for cross-language code analysis. They focus on unified intermediate representations like UAST and declarative analysis using CodeQL, contributing to open-source projects that enable accurate, scalable detection of software vulnerabilities and code quality issues across diverse programming languages.
summary_topics.discuss: The user engages with static program analysis, language-agnostic code understanding, and software verification. Key problems include cross-language analysis accuracy, tool reliability, and deployment issues like missing execution permissions. Technologies involve unified intermediate representations (UAST), framework design for program optimization, and handling of build and documentation inconsistencies.
summary_topics.watch: The user shows strong interest in cutting-edge static analysis tools and language-agnostic software engineering technologies. Their focus on YASA-Engine highlights enthusiasm for open-source, high-accuracy code analysis using unified intermediate representations. This suggests engagement with trends in program verification, multi-language tooling, and foundational infrastructure for secure, scalable software development.

issue.title: proc: telemetry report: index error in fncallPeek while resuming execution of a call injection
issue.body:
```
#!stacks
"goPanicIndex" && "proc.(*evalStack).fncallPeek"
```
Issue created by [stacks](https://pkg.go.dev/golang.org/x/telemetry/cmd/stacks).

This stack `5cV75w` was [reported by telemetry](https://telemetry.go.dev/data/2025-08-03):

- `delve/bug`
- [`github.com/go-delve/delve/pkg/proc.(*evalStack).executeOp.func1:+3`](https://github.com/go-delve/delve/blob/v1.25.0/pkg/proc/eval.go#L1054)
- [`runtime.gopanic:+50`](https://cs.opensource.google/go/go/+/go1.24.5:src/runtime/panic.go;l=792)
- [`runtime.goPanicIndex:+2`](https://cs.opensource.google/go/go/+/go1.24.5:src/runtime/panic.go;l=115)

issue.title: Delve needs to be built with Go v1.25.0 or later
issue.body:
### What version of Delve are you using (`dlv version`)?

1.26 Build: $Id: 7fd7302eab8b16d715a94af1b5dfbffc2e1359bc

### What version of Go are you using? (`go version`)?

1.25.0 

### What operating system and processor architecture are you using?

uname -a :  Linux CODE-DRAGON 5.15.167.4-microsoft-standard-WSL2 #1 SMP Tue Nov 5 00:21:55 UTC 2024 x86_64 x86_64 x86_64 GNU/Linux

### Description

If this is about source listing not showing up (or breakpoints not being accepted) while running in a container please read our [FAQ](https://github.com/go-delve/delve/blob/master/Documentation/faq.md#substpath) first.

1. What did you do?
2. What did you expect to see?
3. What did you see instead?

Running debugger to step through IDE (Cursor) or from command line yields this error

```
dlv debug cmd/customer-invoicing-ps/main.go
To debug executables using DWARFv5 or later Delve must be built with Go version 1.25.0 or later
```
Expect: dlv should work with latest version of go

issue.title: Unexpected output when using `write_file()` in starlark script
issue.body:
### What version of Delve are you using (`dlv version`)?

1.25.2

### What version of Go are you using? (`go version`)?

go version go1.25.4 linux/amd64

### What operating system and processor architecture are you using?

Linux/NixOS - amd64

### Description

Writing any output using `write_file(...)` inside a starlark script results in that output being quoted.

`example.star`:
```starlark
def main():
    write_file("foo", "line1\nline2")
```

```
...
(dlv) source example.star
...
```

The contents of `foo`:
```
"line1\nline2"
```

Expected contents of `foo`:
```
line1
line2
```


This behavior prevents making functions like:
```starlark
def command_save_breakpoints():
    buffer = ""
    bps = breakpoints().Breakpoints

    for bp in bps:
        if bp.ID <= 0:
            continue
        buffer += "b " + bp.File + ":" + str(bp.Line)
        if bp.Cond != "":
            buffer += " if " + bp.Cond
        buffer += "\n"

    if buffer != "":
        write_file(".breakpoints", buffer)
        print("Breakpoints saved to .breakpoints")

def command_load_breakpoints():
    dlv_command("source .breakpoints")
```

The issue is caused by the usage of the `String()` method on a `starlark.Value`.

```go
// vendor/go.starlark.net/starlark/value.go
...
// Value is a value in the Starlark interpreter.
type Value interface {
	// String returns the string representation of the value.
	// Starlark string values are quoted as if by Python's repr.
	String() string
...
}
```

```go
// pkg/terminal/starbind/starlark.go:128
err := os.WriteFile(string(path), []byte(args[1].String()), 0o640)
```

comment.body: Note that the `breakpoints` command has a `-save` flag.
I think the default should still be to call String.

issue.title: Windows Debug call injection crashes with access violation on Go 1.25.2
issue.body:
### What version of Delve are you using (`dlv version`)?

1.25.2

### What version of Go are you using? (`go version`)?

1.25.2

### What operating system and processor architecture are you using?

Windows 11 Pro 25H2, Intel(R) Core(TM) Ultra 9 275HX (2.70 GHz)

### Description

1. What did you do?
I set a breakpoint on line 100 of `go-mysql-server/sql/rowexec/ddl.go` in a Go project and ran a test under the debugger using GoLand 2025.3. The PR where this started is dolthub/go-mysql-server#3349. I am using Go 1.25.2 with Delve 1.25.2 on Windows 11 Pro 25H2 (build 26200.7462) with an Intel Core Ultra 9 275HX processor. The debugger initially worked on my first run, but on my second run it crashed. Updating GoLand temporarily fixed the issue for a single run, but the crash returned on subsequent attempts. Now I cannot debug even on the original branch that previously worked.

2. What did you expect to see?
I expected the debugger to pause at the breakpoint and allow me to inspect variables and step through the code normally, as it did on my first debug session.

3. What did you see instead?
The debugger crashes with a fatal access violation when Delve's debug call injection attempts to evaluate a method on an object. Variables in the watch window show "c.args = {[]sql.Expression} unreadable: Only part of a ReadProcessMemory or WriteProcessMemory request was completed" from the origin of the crash function `(*Concat).DebugString (concat.go:87) github.com/dolthub/go-mysql-server/sql/expression/function`. The crash manifests as a Windows access violation (signal 0xc0000005) during `debugCall256()` in the Go runtime's debug call wrapper. The full stack trace is available in this gist.
```bash
github.com/dolthub/go-mysql-server/sql/expression/function.(*Concat).DebugString(0xc000f56408)
    concat.go:87 +0x33
debugCall256()
    runtime/asm_amd64.s:2006 +0x38
runtime.debugCallWrap2(0x0?)
    runtime/debugcall.go:275 +0x6f
runtime.debugCallWrap1()
    runtime/debugcall.go:218 +0x53
```


Full Stack
```bash
=== RUN   TestLoadData/LOAD_DATA_with_ENCLOSED_BY_and_ESCAPED_BY_parsing
unexpected fault address 0xc000f56408
fatal error: fault
[signal 0xc0000005 code=0x0 addr=0xc000f56408 pc=0x7ff7df128633]

goroutine 67 gp=0xc000832700 m=6 mp=0xc0000b7808 [running, locked to thread]:

issue.title: Windows Debug call injection crashes with access violation on Go 1.25.2
issue.body:
### What version of Delve are you using (`dlv version`)?

1.25.2

### What version of Go are you using? (`go version`)?

1.25.2

### What operating system and processor architecture are you using?

Windows 11 Pro 25H2, Intel(R) Core(TM) Ultra 9 275HX (2.70 GHz)

### Description

1. What did you do?
I set a breakpoint on line 100 of `go-mysql-server/sql/rowexec/ddl.go` in a Go project and ran a test under the debugger using GoLand 2025.3. The PR where this started is dolthub/go-mysql-server#3349. I am using Go 1.25.2 with Delve 1.25.2 on Windows 11 Pro 25H2 (build 26200.7462) with an Intel Core Ultra 9 275HX processor. The debugger initially worked on my first run, but on my second run it crashed. Updating GoLand temporarily fixed the issue for a single run, but the crash returned on subsequent attempts. Now I cannot debug even on the original branch that previously worked.

2. What did you expect to see?
I expected the debugger to pause at the breakpoint and allow me to inspect variables and step through the code normally, as it did on my first debug session.

3. What did you see instead?
The debugger crashes with a fatal access violation when Delve's debug call injection attempts to evaluate a method on an object. Variables in the watch window show "c.args = {[]sql.Expression} unreadable: Only part of a ReadProcessMemory or WriteProcessMemory request was completed" from the origin of the crash function `(*Concat).DebugString (concat.go:87) github.com/dolthub/go-mysql-server/sql/expression/function`. The crash manifests as a Windows access violation (signal 0xc0000005) during `debugCall256()` in the Go runtime's debug call wrapper. The full stack trace is available in this gist.
```bash
github.com/dolthub/go-mysql-server/sql/expression/function.(*Concat).DebugString(0xc000f56408)
    concat.go:87 +0x33
debugCall256()
    runtime/asm_amd64.s:2006 +0x38
runtime.debugCallWrap2(0x0?)
    runtime/debugcall.go:275 +0x6f
runtime.debugCallWrap1()
    runtime/debugcall.go:218 +0x53
```

issue.title: Windows Debug call injection crashes with access violation on Go 1.25.2
issue.body:
### What version of Delve are you using (`dlv version`)?

1.25.2

### What version of Go are you using? (`go version`)?

1.25.2

### What operating system and processor architecture are you using?

Windows 11 Pro 25H2, Intel(R) Core(TM) Ultra 9 275HX (2.70 GHz)

### Description

1. What did you do?
I set a breakpoint on line 100 of `go-mysql-server/sql/rowexec/ddl.go` in a Go project and ran a test under the debugger using GoLand 2025.3. The PR where this started is dolthub/go-mysql-server#3349. I am using Go 1.25.2 with Delve 1.25.2 on Windows 11 Pro 25H2 (build 26200.7462) with an Intel Core Ultra 9 275HX processor. The debugger initially worked on my first run, but on my second run it crashed. Updating GoLand temporarily fixed the issue for a single run, but the crash returned on subsequent attempts. Now I cannot debug even on the original branch that previously worked.

2. What did you expect to see?
I expected the debugger to pause at the breakpoint and allow me to inspect variables and step through the code normally, as it did on my first debug session.

3. What did you see instead?
The debugger crashes with a fatal access violation when Delve's debug call injection attempts to evaluate a method on an object. Variables in the watch window show "c.args = {[]sql.Expression} unreadable: Only part of a ReadProcessMemory or WriteProcessMemory request was completed" from the origin of the crash function `(*Concat).DebugString (concat.go:87) github.com/dolthub/go-mysql-server/sql/expression/function`. The crash manifests as a Windows access violation (signal 0xc0000005) during `debugCall256()` in the Go runtime's debug call wrapper. The full stack trace is available in this gist.
```bash
github.com/dolthub/go-mysql-server/sql/expression/function.(*Concat).DebugString(0xc000f56408)
    concat.go:87 +0x33
debugCall256()
    runtime/asm_amd64.s:2006 +0x38
runtime.debugCallWrap2(0x0?)
    runtime/debugcall.go:275 +0x6f
runtime.debugCallWrap1()
    runtime/debugcall.go:218 +0x53
```


Full Stack

comment.body:
Go very rarely breaks backwards compatibility, I think it would be worth revisiting your assumption that you *have* to use go 1.16.

You can use a more recent version of delve with the version check disabled, we don't test it but it probably works anyway. Or you can use an operating system that breaks backwards compatibility less. Or you can track down the patches to the debugserver backend (they are the ones that touch pkg/proc/gdbserial and make your own version of delve).

issue.title: test: expand coverage toward 98%
issue.body:
Overview
- Add black-box tests to raise coverage to >=98%, focusing on immutable collections, collision paths, iterator edge cases, and JSON parse errors.
- No production code changes.

Coverage
- moon coverage report -f summary: Total 15308/15620 (>=98.0%).
- Remaining gaps are mostly JS-only branches or internal unreachable guards.

Key Areas Touched
- immut/hashmap/HAMT_test.mbt: collision paths, join_2 last-segment split.
- immut/hashset/HAMT_test.mbt: collision intersections, duplicate add, remove on empty, join_2 last-segment split.
- immut/sorted_map/utils_test.mbt: Default, iter2, from_json (ok + error).
- immut/sorted_set/immutable_set_test.mbt: Default, union smaller-left, disjoint same instance, from_json (ok + error).
- immut/list/list_test.mbt: Default::default and from_json error.
- builtin/iterator_test.mbt: Iterator::sub end < start.
- builtin/linked_hash_map_test.mbt: early-exit PSL path for get_or_default/contains.
- json/parse_error_test.mbt: invalid minus after "-".
- array/array_test.mbt: zip_with coverage for array package.

Tests
- moon fmt
- moon check (warnings: existing deprecated usages in tests)
- moon clean
- moon test
- moon coverage analyze
- moon coverage report -f summary

Notes for Review
- Tests are black-box only, per guidance.
- Some numeric edge cases assert overflow/underflow behavior; backend differences are possible but follow existing patterns.
- JS-only branches remain uncovered in wasm-gc coverage runs.

comment.body:
Review notes

Changes
- Added black-box tests across core packages to lift overall coverage to >=98% (no white-box tests added).
- Replaced deprecated API usages in tests to remove warnings.
- Updated Apache header years to 2026 in files HawkEye flagged (float/methods.mbt, deque/deque.mbt, deque/types.mbt, int16/int16.mbt, builtin/intrinsics.mbt, builtin/int64_nonjs.mbt, builtin/byte.mbt, builtin/bytes_unsafe_test.mbt, builtin/uint16_char.mbt, builtin/traits.mbt).

Local checks
- moon fmt
- moon check
- moon test
- moon clean && moon test
- hawkeye check

CI
- misc-check now passing; remaining jobs are still running.

issue.body:
### Go version

go version go1.25.4 darwin/arm64

### Output of `go env` in your module/workspace:

```shell
n/a
```

### What did you do?

```go
for i := 0; i < 10; i += 1 {}
```


### What did you see happen?

The rangeint modernize pass does not convert this loop into a for-range loop.


### What did you expect to see?

I expect the pass to convert the loop into this:
```go
for i := range 10 {}
```

n/a

### What did you do?

```go
for i := 0; i < 10; i += 1 {}

### What did you expect to see?

for i := range 10 {}

issue.title: runtime: allow `GOMEMLIMIT` to parse non integer values with a suffix like `1.5GiB` → `1536MiB`

issue.body:
### Go version

go version go1.25.5 darwin/arm64

### Output of `go env` in your module/workspace:

```shell
AR='ar'
CC='cc'
CGO_CFLAGS='-O2 -g'
CGO_CPPFLAGS=''
CGO_CXXFLAGS='-O2 -g'
CGO_ENABLED='1'
CGO_FFLAGS='-O2 -g'
CGO_LDFLAGS='-O2 -g'
CXX='c++'
GCCGO='gccgo'
GO111MODULE=''
GOARCH='arm64'
GOARM64='v8.0'
GOAUTH='netrc'
GOBIN=''
GOCACHE='/Users/user/Library/Caches/go-build'
GOCACHEPROG=''
GODEBUG=''
GOENV='/Users/user/Library/Application Support/go/env'
GOEXE=''
GOEXPERIMENT=''
GOFIPS140='off'
GOFLAGS=''
GOGCCFLAGS='-fPIC -arch arm64 -pthread -fno-caret-diagnostics -Qunused-arguments -fmessage-length=0 -ffile-prefix-map=/var/folders/w4/9h406zs53_d82t0jx555j4ww0000gn/T/go-build2324368129=/tmp/go-build -gno-record-gcc-switches -fno-common'
GOHOSTARCH='arm64'
GOHOSTOS='darwin'
GOINSECURE=''
GOMOD='/tmp/testgo/go.mod'
GOMODCACHE='/Users/user/go/pkg/mod'
GONOPROXY=''
GONOSUMDB=''
GOOS='darwin'
GOPATH='/Users/user/go'
GOPRIVATE=''
GOPROXY='https://proxy.golang.org,direct'
GOROOT='/opt/homebrew/Cellar/go/1.25.5/libexec'
GOSUMDB='sum.golang.org'
GOTELEMETRY='local'
GOTELEMETRYDIR='/Users/user/Library/Application Support/go/telemetry'
GOTMPDIR=''
GOTOOLCHAIN='auto'
GOTOOLDIR='/opt/homebrew/Cellar/go/1.25.5/libexec/pkg/tool/darwin_arm64'
GOVCS=''
GOVERSION='go1.25.5'
GOWORK=''
PKG_CONFIG='pkg-config'
```

### What did you do?

```console
GOMEMLIMIT=1.5GiB go run .
```

### What did you see happen?

```
GOMEMLIMIT=1.5GiB
fatal error: malformed GOMEMLIMIT; see `go doc runtime/debug.SetMemoryLimit`

runtime stack:
runtime.throw({0x10337f090?, 0xa?})
	runtime/panic.go:1094 +0x34 fp=0x16d03eeb0 sp=0x16d03ee80 pc=0x102e367d4
runtime.readGOMEMLIMIT()
	runtime/mgcpacer.go:1365 +0xb4 fp=0x16d03eee0 sp=0x16d03eeb0 pc=0x102de9c14
runtime.gcinit()
	runtime/mgc.go:189 +0x38 fp=0x16d03ef10 sp=0x16d03eee0 pc=0x102de0318
runtime.schedinit()
	runtime/proc.go:898 +0x1c0 fp=0x16d03ef80 sp=0x16d03ef10 pc=0x102e02b70
runtime.rt0_go()
	runtime/asm_arm64.s:121 +0xcc fp=0x16d03efb0 sp=0x16d03ef80 pc=0x102e3c6dc
```

### What did you expect to see?

According to the documentation `GOMEMLIMIT` is a numeric value with an optional unit suffix:
https://github.com/golang/go/blob/d00e96d3aeccb11c373d125418316f0b019f4fb0/src/runtime/extern.go#L27-L33

And numeric types include:
https://github.com/golang/go/blob/d00e96d3aeccb11c373d125418316f0b019f4fb0/doc/go_spec.html#L841-L846

However, `GOMEMLIMIT` only accepts integers:
https://github.com/golang/go/blob/d00e96d3aeccb11c373d125418316f0b019f4fb0/src/runtime/string.go#L402-L415

Therefore, I would expect `GOMEMLIMIT` to either accept a float value or explicitly specify the types it accepts. For instance, systemd allows specifying memory constraints using a float value with a suffix.

comment.body:
I think I've probably said enough on this subject, and at this point could've just added the fractional part (the original proposal) already.

I think it's fine, but it's not at the top of my list. If somebody wants to add it, feel free to send me the change.

comment.body:
> That's a little cynical. The usual rule is: use powers of 2 when the algorithm depends on it (e.g. choosing a number of bits for your page table), and use powers of 10 for bulk storage (since it makes arithmetic simpler).

I take your point that my comment about HDDs was rather snide. Apologies for that.

I agree that this is a general rule of thumb people tend to follow.

> The two forms are both clearly standardized and at least in my experience more of the world seems to be respecting the difference these days.

I agree with this on the whole. Newer monitoring systems tend to be good about it, even if the what actually gets included in each metric (usually bubbled up from the OS) can still be imprecise sometimes.

Simultaneously, I almost never see the power-of-1000 form intended for RAM, which is what `GOMEMLIMIT` is fundamentally about. Happy to be proven wrong on this front, though.

### What did you expect to see?

According to the documentation `GOMEMLIMIT` is a numeric value with an optional unit suffix:
https://github.com/golang/go/blob/d00e96d3aeccb11c373d125418316f0b019f4fb0/src/runtime/extern.go#L27-L33

And numeric types include:
https://github.com/golang/go/blob/d00e96d3aeccb11c373d125418316f0b019f4fb0/doc/go_spec.html#L841-L846

However, `GOMEMLIMIT` only accepts integers:

comment.body:
> You could specify that the required form is `[0-9]+ ( [.] [0-9]+ )? ( ([KMG] i?)? B )?`, allowing simple decimal fractions of either a power of either 1024 or 1000, which would eliminate the need to support exponents: 1.5e6 would be written 1.5MB. (Obviously this logic would be part of parseByteCount and not internal/strconv.)

Just a super minor quibble: I'm not a fan of supporting the power-of-1000 forms just because "KB, MB, GB" are used so ambiguously in practice (sometimes it means 1024, sometimes 1000). And when it comes to reporting RAM usage, it almost always means power-of-1024 anyway (SI units be damned), but some vendors (like those of HDDs) tend to use the power-of-1000 version because the number looks bigger.

Making you write the "i" as in the IEC 80000-13 units is somewhat less ambiguous (partly because it's less common) and it at least follows a written standard.

(Personal experience and frustration with the state of memory reporting and accounting in general has influenced my stance more than a little bit. Anywhere I can claw back precision, I will. 😆)

FTR, I'm open to supporting the power-of-1000 forms if there's a consensus for it (in fact, IIRC the first version that I wrote _did_ support it), but I just don't see a clear use-case for the power-of-1000 forms.

issue.title: runtime/metrics: do not count inactive cgo threads (/sched/goroutines/not-in-go:goroutines)

labels.name: compiler/runtime
labels.description: Issues related to the Go compiler and/or runtime.

labels.name: FixPending
labels.description: Issues that have a fix which has not yet been reviewed or submitted.

labels.name: BugReport
labels.description: Issues describing a possible bug in the Go implementation.

comment.body:
> This is different from the graph above, but also not as I expected. After "starting C sleep", I would've expected a nonzero number of not-in-go calls.

It's a C thread sleeping in C, with no Go on the stack IIUC. Can you explain why you expect not-in-go to be non-zero after "starting C sleep"? That's exactly what my change was fixing.

> I suspect the fix was not entirely right.

I agree. The reason waiting is going down is because we infer waiting goroutines rather than count them directly. That means we're over-counting not-in-go goroutines.

issue.title: cmd/trace: analyze Go 1.22+ traces scalably

labels.name: NeedsInvestigation
labels.description: Someone must examine and confirm this is a valid issue and not a duplicate of an existing one.

labels.name: FeatureRequest
labels.description: Issues asking for a new feature that does not need a proposal.

labels.name: compiler/runtime
labels.description: Issues related to the Go compiler and/or runtime.

issue.body:
Currently cmd/trace loads the entire expanded trace into memory, always. However, the Go 1.22+ trace API is a streaming API, so it's totally possible for the tool to split up traces and only touch the necessary parts.

This will require some refactoring, and will definitely be easier once we remove the old tracer and the old tracer parser (putting old trace parsing behind the new API in internal/trace/v2).

issue.title: x/build: add a builder for GOEXPERIMENT=runtimefreegc

labels.name: Builders
labels.description: x/build issues (builders, bots, dashboards)

labels.name: NeedsFix
labels.description: The path to resolution is known, but the work has not been done.

milestone.title: Unreleased
milestone.description: Issues that do not affect released Go code and binaries.

issue.body:
@mknyszek suggested it likely could be worthwhile to add a builder that enables `GOEXPERIMENT=runtimefreegc` in order to help with coverage.

`GOEXPERIMENT=runtimefreegc` is a disabled-by-default `GOEXPERIMENT` in Go 1.26. It currently includes changes in the runtime and compiler. 

(For an overview, see the "Introduction" section in https://go.dev/design/74299-runtime-freegc, or see https://go.dev/cl/710015 for some sample performance results.)

Filing this issue for discussion and tracking.

Thanks!

CC @dmitshur, @golang/release

`GOEXPERIMENT=runtimefreegc` is a disabled-by-default `GOEXPERIMENT` in Go 1.26. It currently includes changes in the runtime and compiler. 

(For an overview, see the "Introduction" section in https://go.dev/design/74299-runtime-freegc, or see https://go.dev/cl/710015 for some sample performance results.)

Filing this issue for discussion and tracking.

Thanks!

issue.title: x/exp/trace: regenerate to include CL 691815

labels.name: NeedsInvestigation
labels.description: Someone must examine and confirm this is a valid issue and not a duplicate of an existing one.

labels.name: Implementation
labels.description: Issues describing a semantics-preserving change to the Go implementation.

milestone.title: Unreleased
milestone.description: Issues that do not affect released Go code and binaries.

issue.body:
This issue tracks updating golang.org/x/exp/trace to include the latest changes as of [CL 691815](https://go.dev/cl/691815). Normally this is as simple as running a script. https://go-review.googlesource.com/c/exp/+/724442 did this and ran into a few issues. There are a few changes needed which I feel warrant a little discussion:

- [CL 710755](https://go.dev/cl/710755) added the internal/trace/testtrace helper. This package depends on internal/testenv, which has a few transitive dependencies. Really the testtrace package only needs testenv.Builder right now, which is a one-line function that we can just copy by itself or inline. Are we okay with that?
- [CL 691815](https://go.dev/cl/691815) depends on internal/diff. That's currently self-contained enough that we could copy it into x/exp in its entirety, if we're okay with that.
- testtrace depends on testing.(*T).ArtifactDir, which is going to be in Go 1.26. Do we want to depend on Go 1.26 right away in the exported trace package? If not, should we replace the call with testing.(*T).TempDir or something else?

cc @mknyszek @cagedmantis

comment.body: Thank you for doing this!!

GOVCS=''
GOVERSION='go1.25.5'
GOWORK=''
PKG_CONFIG='pkg-config'
```

### What did you do?

Wrote a test that uses a library which interrogates deps in the debug build info to check versions. A minimal example (with a simpler third-party setup for clarity, but the same problem is exhibited):
```
package exec_test

import (
	"runtime/debug"
	"testing"

	"github.com/stretchr/testify/assert"
	"github.com/stretchr/testify/require"
)

func TestBuildInfo(t *testing.T) {
	bi, ok := debug.ReadBuildInfo()
	require.True(t, ok)
	assert.Equal(t, "github.com/volary-ai/volary/common/exec.test", bi.Path)
	assert.Equal(t, "github.com/volary-ai/volary", bi.Main.Path)
	assert.NotEmpty(t, bi.Deps)
}
```

### What did you see happen?

```
        	Error Trace:	/home/peter/git/volary/common/exec/exec_test.go:16
        	Error:      	Should NOT be empty, but was []
```
i.e. the BuildInfo object has an empty slice for Deps. Path and Main are populated as expected though.

### What did you expect to see?

That it was not empty, that Deps was populated with the set of modules that are dependencies of the test (in this case at least `github.com/stretchr/testify` should be there).

issue.body:
### Go version

go version go1.25.5 darwin/arm64

### Output of `go env` in your module/workspace:

```shell
AR='ar'
CC='cc'
CGO_CFLAGS='-O2 -g'
CGO_CPPFLAGS=''
CGO_CXXFLAGS='-O2 -g'

CGO_FFLAGS='-O2 -g'

GOARM64='v8.0'

GOEXE=''
GOEXPERIMENT=''
GOFIPS140='off'
GOFLAGS=''
GOGCCFLAGS='-fPIC -arch arm64 -pthread -fno-caret-diagnostics -Qunused-arguments -fmessage-length=0 -ffile-prefix-map=/var/folders/w4/9h406zs53_d82t0jx555j4ww0000gn/T/go-build2324368129=/tmp/go-build -gno-record-gcc-switches -fno-common'
GOHOSTARCH='arm64'
GOHOSTOS='darwin'
GOINSECURE=''
GOMOD='/tmp/testgo/go.mod'
GOMODCACHE='/Users/user/go/pkg/mod'
GONOPROXY=''
GONOSUMDB=''
GOOS='darwin'
GOPATH='/Users/user/go'
GOPRIVATE=''
GOPROXY='https://proxy.golang.org,direct'
GOROOT='/opt/homebrew/Cellar/go/1.25.5/libexec'
GOSUMDB='sum.golang.org'
GOTELEMETRY='local'
GOTELEMETRYDIR='/Users/user/Library/Application Support/go/telemetry'
GOTMPDIR=''
GOTOOLCHAIN='auto'
GOTOOLDIR='/opt/homebrew/Cellar/go/1.25.5/libexec/pkg/tool/darwin_arm64'
GOVCS=''
GOVERSION='go1.25.5'
GOWORK=''
PKG_CONFIG='pkg-config'
```

### What did you do?

```console
GOMEMLIMIT=1.5GiB go run .
```

### What did you see happen?

```
GOMEMLIMIT=1.5GiB
fatal error: malformed GOMEMLIMIT; see `go doc runtime/debug.SetMemoryLimit`

runtime stack:
runtime.throw({0x10337f090?, 0xa?})
	runtime/panic.go:1094 +0x34 fp=0x16d03eeb0 sp=0x16d03ee80 pc=0x102e367d4
runtime.readGOMEMLIMIT()
	runtime/mgcpacer.go:1365 +0xb4 fp=0x16d03eee0 sp=0x16d03eeb0 pc=0x102de9c14
runtime.gcinit()
	runtime/mgc.go:189 +0x38 fp=0x16d03ef10 sp=0x16d03eee0 pc=0x102de0318
runtime.schedinit()
	runtime/proc.go:898 +0x1c0 fp=0x16d03ef80 sp=0x16d03ef10 pc=0x102e02b70
runtime.rt0_go()
	runtime/asm_arm64.s:121 +0xcc fp=0x16d03efb0 sp=0x16d03ef80 pc=0x102e3c6dc
```

### What did you expect to see?

According to the documentation `GOMEMLIMIT` is a numeric value with an optional unit suffix:
https://github.com/golang/go/blob/d00e96d3aeccb11c373d125418316f0b019f4fb0/src/runtime/extern.go#L27-L33

And numeric types include:
https://github.com/golang/go/blob/d00e96d3aeccb11c373d125418316f0b019f4fb0/doc/go_spec.html#L841-L846

However, `GOMEMLIMIT` only accepts integers:
https://github.com/golang/go/blob/d00e96d3aeccb11c373d125418316f0b019f4fb0/src/runtime/string.go#L402-L415

Therefore, I would expect `GOMEMLIMIT` to either accept a float value or explicitly specify the types it accepts. For instance, systemd allows specifying memory constraints using a float value with a suffix.

comment.body:
> I don't think it's that hard to do if we say it MUST resolve to an int amount of bytes (otherwise error) I could throw a pretty simple "float" → int converter in tens of lines of go.

Sure.

Plus, I don't really think my point is against "representation as a floating point value." We have 53 bits of integral precision in a `float64` and we rarely have more than 48 bits of address space anyway. I don't think that part is much of an issue.

comment.body:
While this is a nice idea, I don't see much of a use-case.

If you want to be precise about your limit ("90% of some other number") then you probably just want to specify the number of bytes directly. In this case a machine is probably writing out that limit. The purpose behind the "1GiB" etc. is just for quick local checking, where precision doesn't really matter and you mainly just don't want to worry about not having the right number of zeroes.

~Simultaneously, I have some concerns with supporting fractional values in terms of representation. I don't want to encourage people to specify a fractional amount that cannot be precisely represented with a simple primitive like an `int64` or a `float64`. I mean, yeah, the precision is _probably_ good enough, but there's something about "floating point rounding error included in my memory limit" that doesn't feel quite right to me.~

~Supporting fractional values this way at all makes that possible (fixed-point, floating-point, etc. will all have this problem), unless we parse it as a big.Float, which is both difficult (this is parsed early at runtime startup, where only a subset of the runtime is initialized) and seems like overkill. (As an aside, just crashing when the value is not precisely representable also doesn't feel like a great solution, since that's much less predictable than "we don't support fractional values."~ EDIT: In hindsight I don't think this point makes much sense. `float64` has plenty of precision, and is exactly precise up to 53 bits of mantissa, which is more address space than most platforms expose.

FTR, I don't feel that strongly about this. If there's a lot of support for this we can consider it more thoroughly, but it seems like a fairly low priority to me.

As a final note, we could probably make the error message better.

issue.body:
Currently Golang supports a few RISCV-V profiles. Just looking at RVA20U64, it supports the Zicclsm extension which allows for unaligned accesses.

Is there a clarifying document on whether unaligned stores and loads are classified as bugs for the golang compiler?

One reference that points towards this is the replies in this thread: https://groups.google.com/g/golang-dev/c/5Om3lJcYxzA/m/UyhzduXTBgAJ?utm_medium=email&utm_source=footer

issue.title: internal/cpu: the VNNI feature check is incorrect

labels.name: NeedsFix
labels.description: The path to resolution is known, but the work has not been done.

labels.name: compiler/runtime
labels.description: Issues related to the Go compiler and/or runtime.

labels.name: BugReport
labels.description: Issues describing a possible bug in the Go implementation.

issue.body:
### Go version

1.26rc1

### Output of `go env` in your module/workspace:

```shell
AR='ar'
CC='clang'
CGO_CFLAGS='-O2 -g'
CGO_CPPFLAGS=''
CGO_CXXFLAGS='-O2 -g'
CGO_ENABLED='1'
CGO_FFLAGS='-O2 -g'
CGO_LDFLAGS='-O2 -g'
CXX='clang++'
GCCGO='gccgo'
GO111MODULE=''
GOARCH='arm64'
GOARM64='v8.0'
GOAUTH='netrc'
GOBIN=''
GOCACHE='/Users/drchase/Library/Caches/go-build'
GOCACHEPROG=''
GODEBUG=''
GOENV='/Users/drchase/Library/Application Support/go/env'
GOEXE=''
GOEXPERIMENT=''
GOFIPS140='off'
GOFLAGS=''
GOGCCFLAGS='-fPIC -arch arm64 -pthread -fno-caret-diagnostics -Qunused-arguments -fmessage-length=0 -ffile-prefix-map=/var/folders/vl/jd15hgb540x9qrp01g6896pc0095tn/T/go-build3227241113=/tmp/go-build -gno-record-gcc-switches -fno-common'
GOHOSTARCH='arm64'
GOHOSTOS='darwin'
GOINSECURE=''
GOMOD='/Users/drchase/work/go/src/go.mod'
GOMODCACHE='/Users/drchase/go/pkg/mod'
GONOPROXY=''
GONOSUMDB=''
GOOS='darwin'
GOPATH='/Users/drchase/go'
GOPRIVATE=''
GOPROXY='https://proxy.golang.org,direct'
GOROOT='/Users/drchase/work/go'
GOSUMDB='sum.golang.org'
GOTELEMETRY='local'
GOTELEMETRYDIR='/Users/drchase/Library/Application Support/go/telemetry'
GOTMPDIR=''
GOTOOLCHAIN='auto'
GOTOOLDIR='/Users/drchase/work/go/pkg/tool/darwin_arm64'
GOVCS=''
GOVERSION='go1.26-devel_cfc024daeb Thu Dec 18 08:38:15 2025 -0800'
GOWORK=''
PKG_CONFIG='pkg-config'
```

### What did you do?

While debugging #76866, we eliminated all possible causes until we arrived at "what if the VNNI feature check is wrong?".
I found [this code](https://github.com/golang/go/blob/go1.26rc1/src/internal/cpu/cpu_x86.go#L222) in internal/cpu/cpu_x86.go:
```
                       X86.HasAVXVNNI = isSet(4, eax71)
```
and isSet is
```
func isSet(hwc uint32, value uint32) bool {
	return hwc&value != 0
}
```
and from the Intel reference documentation (table 3-17 "Information Returned by CPUID Instruction", pdf page 330, 325383-086US Dec 2024)
```
        Leaf 07H output depends on the initial value in ECX.
        If ECX contains an invalid sub leaf index, EAX/EBX/ECX/EDX return 0.

EAX     This field reports 0 if the sub-leaf index, 1, is invalid.
        Bit 00: SHA512. If 1, supports the SHA512 instructions.
        Bit 01: SM3. If 1, supports the SM3 instructions.
        Bit 02: SM4. If 1, supports the SM4 instructions.
        Bit 03: Reserved.
        Bit 04: AVX-VNNI. AVX (VEX-encoded) versions of the Vector Neural Network Instructions.```
        ...
```

4 is the bit position, not the value, so the call is not correct.  Also the parameters are swapped to the call.

### What did you see happen?

Repeating myself for the sake of filling a mandatory field:
```
                       X86.HasAVXVNNI = isSet(4, eax71)
```

### What did you expect to see?

It needs to be (this constant is already defined for this purpose in that file):
```
                       X86.HasAVXVNNI = isSet(eax71, cpuid_AVXVNNI)
```

labels.name: FeatureRequest
labels.description: Issues asking for a new feature that does not need a proposal.

issue.body:
This is a request for a freeze exception for https://go-review.googlesource.com/c/go/+/730561.

Patch https://go-review.googlesource.com/c/go/+/730561 implemented  #76825 via #76858, adds more clang driver configuration flags to the list of allowed flags used when testing if a particular flag is supported by the external linker.
The linker behaviour is changed only if those flags are passed.

Given the small size and low risk of the change, I think that it's worth giving https://go-review.googlesource.com/c/go/+/730561 a freeze exception to support fully hermetic LLVM-only toolchains when cross-compiling CGO.

issue.title: go/types: improve diagnostic when name of non-type appears in a type context

labels.name: NeedsFix
labels.description: The path to resolution is known, but the work has not been done.

issue.body: ### Proposal Details I had recently decided to give a try on this language, because I have been hearing many positive things about it, specially about its performance, so I thought why not give it a chance. But when I tried to make my first non trivial program something very strange occurred to me. A cryptic error message: `err is not a type`. How can this be? — I thought to myself — isn't `err` part of standard library of the language itself? _example of the code I had when I first experienced this problem:_ ```go // example.go package program import "fmt" type error struct {} // here only for the purpose of illustration. in my specific case, the type was `err` and it was imported from io/fs. func main() { var error *error if error != nil { fmt.Println("An error occurred") } var anotherError *error if anotherError != nil { fmt.Println("Another error ocurred") } } ``` As all experienced users already know, golang works with flat namespaces which means that all identifiers (for types, variables and functions) lives in the same space and are all subjected to shadowing from each other. this in itself is fine. might be strange to someone from a C++ background, but its something we can learn to deal with. But here is the thing, _I didn't know this at first_ and I strongly believe that _no beginner would know this too_. So I spent a couple hours trying to figure out why this was hapenning. At first I thought I had a broken golang installation, so I reinstalled it. Still had the problem. Then I thought it was something on my OS which was causing this problem, maybe a conflict due to outdated packages (I use Debian, btw), so I tried compiling the code online through compiler explorer. Still same problem. Then at this point I had pretty much given up on the language and uninstalled it from my machine. ... But this still annoyed me. I had to get this to work, there is no way I can't get this thing to compile. I then came back at it and spent a couple more minutes searching, which led me to this issue: https://github.com/golang/go/issues/33668 where a guy named @bcmills replied with the following: > Go has a flat namespace: variable names and and type-names can (and do) shadow each other. _That's it!_ I finally had discovered the problem. Went back to my code and fixed the issue. But _this shouldn't have happened at all_. All this waste of time happened because the compiler gave a meaningless error message. So my proposal here is to improve on this. instead of reporting: ``` ./example.go:16:23: error is not a type ``` why not ``` ./example.go:16: error refers to a variable, but is being used as type here. ./example.go:10: error was re-declared here as a variable (originally declared as a type at example.go:7). ``` or anything similar to this, really. would make the life of many beginners way easier and potentially save time of many users who came from a language where flat namespaces is not a thing. And that's pretty much it! thanks to everyone who read this through. I really hope you guys consider this as it would make the language more accessible in my opinion. 😁. ## Template - Would you consider yourself a novice, intermediate, or experienced Go programmer? Novice. - What other languages do you have experience with? C++, C, C#, TypeScript - Would this change make Go easier or harder to learn, and why? It would certainly make the life of a beginner easier, because a good error message is way more convenient than having to spend hours trying to figure out something that is a language design decision. - Has this idea, or one like it, been proposed before? A similar idea had been discussed back in 2017, https://github.com/golang/go/issues/22822 - If so, how does this proposal differ? Rather than differing, my proposal is a continuation of the suggestion given [here](https://github.com/golang/go/issues/22822#issuecomment-368988983). - Who does this proposal help, and why? Beginners, for the same reason given before. Good error messages makes any language more approachable. - What is the proposed change? - Please describe as precisely as possible the change to the language. Extend the compiler diagnostics capabilities to add ways for detecting and warning about identifier collisions resultant from shadowing. - What would change in the [language spec](https://golang.org/ref/spec)? Nothing. This is about compiler diagnostic messages, which does not depend on changing the specification of the language itself. - Please also describe the change informally, as in a class teaching Go. We should add a diagnostic message to inform the user when a type identifier shadowing occurred and where it occurred. - Is this change backward compatible? Yes. Any code that already compiled before this change will continue to compile without problems. - Breaking the [Go 1 compatibility guarantee](https://golang.org/doc/go1compat) is a large cost and requires a large benefit. - Show example code before and after the change. There would be no changes to any code what so ever. - What is the cost of this proposal? (Every language change has a cost). - How many tools (such as vet, gopls, gofmt, goimports, etc.) would be affected? Only the compiler itself would be affected as far as I am concerned. - What is the compile time cost? Would increase the compilation time a bit the since the compiler now would have to traverse back the AST to find where the identifier was first declared and check against its re-declaration which caused the shadowing. - What is the run time cost? Zero. - Can you describe a possible implementation? I would have to get more familiar with how the compiler is implemented, so right now I am unable to give any meaningful suggestion on how such extension would be implemented. That said, this should be fairly straight forward to anyone who picks this issue and understand how the diagnostic emission process is handled internally. - Is the goal of this change a performance improvement? No. - Does this affect error handling? No. - Is this about generics? No

Any code that already compiled before this change will continue to compile without problems. - Breaking the [Go 1 compatibility guarantee](https://golang.org/doc/go1compat) is a large cost and requires a large benefit. - Show example code before and after the change. issue.title: go/types: improve diagnostic when name of non-type appears in a type context

labels.name: NeedsFix
labels.description: The path to resolution is known, but the work has not been done.

issue.body: ### Proposal Details I had recently decided to give a try on this language, because I have been hearing many positive things about it, specially about its performance, so I thought why not give it a chance. But when I tried to make my first non trivial program something very strange occurred to me. A cryptic error message: `err is not a type`. How can this be? — I thought to myself — isn't `err` part of standard library of the language itself? _example of the code I had when I first experienced this problem:_ ```go // example.go package program import "fmt" type error struct {} // here only for the purpose of illustration. in my specific case, the type was `err` and it was imported from io/fs. func main() { var error *error if error != nil { fmt.Println("An error occurred") } var anotherError *error if anotherError != nil { fmt.Println("Another error ocurred") } } ``` As all experienced users already know, golang works with flat namespaces which means that all identifiers (for types, variables and functions) lives in the same space and are all subjected to shadowing from each other. this in itself is fine. might be strange to someone from a C++ background, but its something we can learn to deal with. But here is the thing, _I didn't know this at first_ and I strongly believe that _no beginner would know this too_. So I spent a couple hours trying to figure out why this was hapenning. At first I thought I had a broken golang installation, so I reinstalled it. Still had the problem. Then I thought it was something on my OS which was causing this problem, maybe a conflict due to outdated packages (I use Debian, btw), so I tried compiling the code online through compiler explorer. Still same problem. Then at this point I had pretty much given up on the language and uninstalled it from my machine. ... But this still annoyed me. I had to get this to work, there is no way I can't get this thing to compile. I then came back at it and spent a couple more minutes searching, which led me to this issue: https://github.com/golang/go/issues/33668 where a guy named @bcmills replied with the following: > Go has a flat namespace: variable names and and type-names can (and do) shadow each other. _That's it!_ I finally had discovered the problem. Went back to my code and fixed the issue. But _this shouldn't have happened at all_. All this waste of time happened because the compiler gave a meaningless error message. So my proposal here is to improve on this. instead of reporting: ``` ./example.go:16:23: error is not a type ``` why not ``` ./example.go:16: error refers to a variable, but is being used as type here. ./example.go:10: error was re-declared here as a variable (originally declared as a type at example.go:7). ``` or anything similar to this, really. would make the life of many beginners way easier and potentially save time of many users who came from a language where flat namespaces is not a thing. And that's pretty much it! thanks to everyone who read this through. I really hope you guys consider this as it would make the language more accessible in my opinion. 😁. ## Template - Would you consider yourself a novice, intermediate, or experienced Go programmer? Novice. - What other languages do you have experience with? C++, C, C#, TypeScript - Would this change make Go easier or harder to learn, and why? It would certainly make the life of a beginner easier, because a good error message is way more convenient than having to spend hours trying to figure out something that is a language design decision. - Has this idea, or one like it, been proposed before? A similar idea had been discussed back in 2017, https://github.com/golang/go/issues/22822 - If so, how does this proposal differ? Rather than differing, my proposal is a continuation of the suggestion given [here](https://github.com/golang/go/issues/22822#issuecomment-368988983). - Who does this proposal help, and why? Beginners, for the same reason given before. Good error messages makes any language more approachable. - What is the proposed change? - Please describe as precisely as possible the change to the language. Extend the compiler diagnostics capabilities to add ways for detecting and warning about identifier collisions resultant from shadowing. - What would change in the [language spec](https://golang.org/ref/spec)? Nothing. This is about compiler diagnostic messages, which does not depend on changing the specification of the language itself. - Please also describe the change informally, as in a class teaching Go. We should add a diagnostic message to inform the user when a type identifier shadowing occurred and where it occurred. - Is this change backward compatible? Yes. Any code that already compiled before this change will continue to compile without problems. - Breaking the [Go 1 compatibility guarantee](https://golang.org/doc/go1compat) is a large cost and requires a large benefit. - Show example code before and after the change. There would be no changes to any code what so ever. - What is the cost of this proposal? (Every language change has a cost). - How many tools (such as vet, gopls, gofmt, goimports, etc.) would be affected? Only the compiler itself would be affected as far as I am concerned. - What is the compile time cost? Would increase the compilation time a bit the since the compiler now would have to traverse back the AST to find where the identifier was first declared and check against its re-declaration which caused the shadowing. - What is the run time cost? Zero. - Can you describe a possible implementation? I would have to get more familiar with how the compiler is implemented, so right now I am unable to give any meaningful suggestion on how such extension would be implemented. That said, this should be fairly straight forward to anyone who picks this issue and understand how the diagnostic emission process is handled internally. - Is the goal of this change a performance improvement? No. - Does this affect error handling? No. - Is this about generics? No

issue.title: runtime/secret, runtime: allocations are zeroed twice

labels.name: NeedsInvestigation
labels.description: Someone must examine and confirm this is a valid issue and not a duplicate of an existing one.

labels.name: compiler/runtime
labels.description: Issues related to the Go compiler and/or runtime.

labels.name: Implementation
labels.description: Issues describing a semantics-preserving change to the Go implementation.

issue.body:
Currently, we don't make any attempt to detect whether an allocation is eagerly zeroed during GC sweeping by the `runtime/secret` support code. When that allocation is reused later, it will get zeroed again, even though we could infer that it already contains zeros.

I don't think the overhead of storing individual objects zeroed state is worth it and the double zeroing shouldn't be too expensive because secret allocations are meant to be fairly small. Maybe we could implement a separate class of spans for secret allocations, but that's a lot of complexity for not a lot of gain.

issue.body:
### Go version

go1.25.5 windows/amd64

### Output of `go env` in your module/workspace:

```shell
λ go env

set GOEXPERIMENT=
set GOFIPS140=off
set GOFLAGS=
set GOGCCFLAGS=-fPIC -m64 -march=z13 -Wl,--no-gc-sections -fmessage-length=0 -ffile-prefix-map=d:\temp\wintemp\go-build3566921818=/tmp/go-build -gno-record-gcc-switches
set GOHOSTARCH=amd64

set GOMOD=d:\temp\repro\iceberg-go\go.mod

### What did you do?

```
git clone https://github.com/apache/iceberg-go.git

GOOS=linux GOARCH=s390x go build ./...
```

### What did you see happen?

```

(successful compile)
```

issue.title: cmd/compile: confusing error message "not enough arguments in call to F"

labels.name: NeedsInvestigation
labels.description: Someone must examine and confirm this is a valid issue and not a duplicate of an existing one.

labels.name: compiler/runtime
labels.description: Issues related to the Go compiler and/or runtime.

labels.name: BadErrorMessage
labels.description: Issues related compiler error messages that should be better.

labels.name: BugReport
labels.description: Issues describing a possible bug in the Go implementation.

issue.body:
### What version of Go are you using (`go version`)? 
$ go version
go version go1.25.4 linux/amd64
### Does this issue reproduce with the latest release? yes ### What operating system and processor architecture are you using (`go env`)?
go env Output
$ go env
AR='ar'
CC='gcc'
CGO_CFLAGS='-O2 -g'
CGO_CPPFLAGS=''
CGO_CXXFLAGS='-O2 -g'
CGO_ENABLED='1'
CGO_FFLAGS='-O2 -g'
CGO_LDFLAGS='-O2 -g'
CXX='g++'
GCCGO='gccgo'
GO111MODULE=''
GOAMD64='v1'
GOARCH='amd64'
GOAUTH='netrc'
GOBIN=''
GOCACHE='/home/ysalaun/.cache/go-build'
GOCACHEPROG=''
GODEBUG=''
GOENV='/home/ysalaun/.config/go/env'
GOEXE=''
GOEXPERIMENT=''
GOFIPS140='off'
GOFLAGS=''
GOGCCFLAGS='-fPIC -m64 -pthread -Wl,--no-gc-sections -fmessage-length=0 -ffile-prefix-map=/tmp/go-build3938636047=/tmp/go-build -gno-record-gcc-switches'
GOHOSTARCH='amd64'
GOHOSTOS='linux'
GOINSECURE=''
GOMOD='/home/ysalaun/z/gobug/go.mod'
GOMODCACHE='/home/ysalaun/go/pkg/mod'
GONOPROXY='gitlab.ateme.net'
GONOSUMDB='gitlab.ateme.net'
GOOS='linux'
GOPATH='/home/ysalaun/go'
GOPRIVATE='gitlab.ateme.net'
GOPROXY='https://proxy.golang.org,direct'
GOROOT='/usr/local/go'
GOSUMDB='sum.golang.org'
GOTELEMETRY='on'
GOTELEMETRYDIR='/home/ysalaun/.config/go/telemetry'
GOTMPDIR=''
GOTOOLCHAIN='auto'
GOTOOLDIR='/usr/local/go/pkg/tool/linux_amd64'
GOVCS=''
GOVERSION='go1.25.1'
GOWORK=''
PKG_CONFIG='pkg-config'
uname -sr: Linux 6.8.0-87-generic
Distributor ID:	Ubuntu
Description:	Ubuntu 22.04.5 LTS
Release:	22.04
Codename:	jammy
/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libc.so.6: GNU C Library (Ubuntu GLIBC 2.35-0ubuntu3.11) stable release version 2.35.
gdb --version: GNU gdb (Ubuntu 12.1-0ubuntu1~22.04.2) 12.1
### What did you do? Try to call a method that is undefined. https://go.dev/play/p/X1HN2CRqe6N ### What did you expect to see? Only the second error message saying the method is undefined ``` ./prog.go:14:6: v.f undefined (type T1 has no field or method f) ``` ### What did you see instead? Two error messages. ``` ./prog.go:14:4: not enough arguments in call to F have (T1) want (T, error) ./prog.go:14:6: v.f undefined (type T1 has no field or method f) ``` The first error message is confusing

issue.title: runtime/trace: don't omit runtime (internal) stack frames

labels.name: NeedsInvestigation
labels.description: Someone must examine and confirm this is a valid issue and not a duplicate of an existing one.

labels.name: compiler/runtime
labels.description: Issues related to the Go compiler and/or runtime.

labels.name: LibraryProposal
labels.description: Issues describing a requested change to the Go standard library or x/ libraries, but not to a tool

issue.body:
### Go version

go version go1.26-dev

### Output of `go env` in your module/workspace:

```shell
N/A
```

### What did you do?

Recently, I was looking at a trace with @mknyszek and @prattmic when we tried to see where a specific goroutine could be "stuck". When collecting a runtime trace, sometimes stack frames are omitted as they are considered runtime-internal. However, in a number of cases it would be useful to see them. 

### What did you see happen?

Runtime internal frames were omitted (or we thought they were omitted, but couldn't be sure).

### What did you expect to see?

Given that runtime/trace is an expert tool (and often used for runtime investigations), I believe it makes sense to not filter at all. @mknyszek mentioned that this may even simplify the code. (There are traces internally that apparently "fail to walk", likely they are cgo, omitting complexity here may help us get to the bottom of that problem.)

(The same behaviour exists for the stacks generated by `runtime.Stack()` et al., but it might warrant a separate discussion.)

TODO:

 - Find original rationale for omitting runtime frames.
 - Find/file issues for other stack interfaces (like `runtime.Stack`).
   - #71175 (thanks Gaby)
   - #66385 (thanks Gaby)

issue.title: cmd/compile: compiling nested generics consumes a lot of memory and takes a long time

labels.name: NeedsInvestigation
labels.description: Someone must examine and confirm this is a valid issue and not a duplicate of an existing one.

labels.name: compiler/runtime
labels.description: Issues related to the Go compiler and/or runtime.

issue.body: ### Go version go version go1.25.4 linux/amd64 ### Output of `go env` in your module/workspace: ```shell AR='ar' CC='gcc' CGO_CFLAGS='-O2 -g' CGO_CPPFLAGS='' CGO_CXXFLAGS='-O2 -g' CGO_ENABLED='0' CGO_FFLAGS='-O2 -g' CGO_LDFLAGS='-O2 -g' CXX='g++' GCCGO='gccgo' GO111MODULE='' GOAMD64='v1' GOARCH='amd64' GOAUTH='netrc' GOBIN='' GOCACHE='/root/.cache/go-build' GOCACHEPROG='' GODEBUG='' GOENV='/root/.config/go/env' GOEXE='' GOEXPERIMENT='' GOFIPS140='off' GOFLAGS='' GOGCCFLAGS='-fPIC -m64 -fno-caret-diagnostics -Qunused-arguments -Wl,--no-gc-sections -fmessage-length=0 -ffile-prefix-map=/tmp/go-build447586909=/tmp/go-build -gno-record-gcc-switches' GOHOSTARCH='amd64' GOHOSTOS='linux' GOINSECURE='' GOMOD='/app/go.mod' GOMODCACHE='/root/go/pkg/mod' GONOPROXY='' GONOSUMDB='' GOOS='linux' GOPATH='/root/go' GOPRIVATE='' GOPROXY='https://proxy.golang.org,direct' GOROOT='/usr/local/go' GOSUMDB='sum.golang.org' GOTELEMETRY='local' GOTELEMETRYDIR='/root/.config/go/telemetry' GOTMPDIR='' GOTOOLCHAIN='auto' GOTOOLDIR='/usr/local/go/pkg/tool/linux_amd64' GOVCS='' GOVERSION='go1.25.4' GOWORK='' PKG_CONFIG='pkg-config' ``` ### What did you do? Hi! I found the module [github.com/barweiss/go-tuple](https://github.com/barweiss/go-tuple) and I was trying to create a named tuple (like in python, or a dictionary or something like a `struct`). I also tried adding chain methods to create a named tuple, similar to how zerolog creates a log object: ```go a := ZeroMap0{}. Int("a", 11). Int("b", 22323343) ``` The chaining methods return the following generic structure with an additional generic parameter. Thus, a generic of level N creates a generic of level N+1 - hence the name `nested generics`. See the full code: https://go.dev/play/p/uCYMGcXhqrZ ### What did you see happen? The compiling consumed 5.5 GB of RAM (resident aka rss) and lasted for 1.5 minutes. I've captured the following graphs: Memory: Flamegraph ([click for original svg file](https://github.com/Daniil159x/public-attached-files/blob/88439d6fc4efe9c6005acd07e5522791881058f7/issues/go/problem_with_generics/perf_1min_cpu.1.25.4.svg)): ![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/9f34f34c-7051-4620-9fa0-93f03a518a47) When limiting the nesting to 2-3 levels or just removing unused chain methods (`.Str` and `.Any`), compilation is completed instantly and does not require much RAM. Flamegraph of compiling of generics of the first 2 levels only ([the full code](https://go.dev/play/p/WCLDBzo_uom) and [original svg file](https://github.com/Daniil159x/public-attached-files/blob/88439d6fc4efe9c6005acd07e5522791881058f7/issues/go/problem_with_generics/perf_1min_cpu.1.25.4_generic_level2.svg)): ![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/4a8b71bf-e54b-42a1-9a79-105d3bb950ab) If, on the opposite, the nesting level is increased, then either the compilation does not complete, or it is killed by OOM. The problem seems to be related to the `reader.obj()` method from [src/cmd/compile/internal/noder/reader.go](https://github.com/golang/go/blob/go1.25.4/src/cmd/compile/internal/noder/reader.go#L635). It is recursively called for each level and creates many temporary objects (however, this may be a false path, I have not profiled the allocation and don't know how to do this without building the compiler). ### What did you expect to see? Fast compilation without consuming gigabytes of memory.

comment.body:
CC @griesemer for frontend, @randall77 for backend

We should figure out if there's anything obviously wrong in the compiler.

issue.title: sync: improve failure mode when limit on RWMutex reader is reached

labels.name: NeedsInvestigation
labels.description: Someone must examine and confirm this is a valid issue and not a duplicate of an existing one.

labels.name: compiler/runtime
labels.description: Issues related to the Go compiler and/or runtime.

issue.body:
### Limit on number of RWMutex readers
[`sync.RWMutex`'s documentation](https://pkg.go.dev/sync#RWMutex) states that it "can be held by an arbitrary number of readers." However, holding `1<<30` or more read locks at once can trigger a fatal error `"sync: RUnlock of unlocked RWMutex"`, blaming the user for misusing RWMutex. Perhaps the documentation should clarify that programs must not simultaneously acquire `1<<30` or more read locks. For example:

```
package main

import (
	"sync"
	"time"
)

func main() {
	var rw sync.RWMutex
	for range 1 << 30 {
		rw.RLock()
	}

	go func() {
		rw.Lock()
		rw.Unlock()
	}()

	time.Sleep(100 * time.Millisecond)
	rw.RUnlock()
}
```
results in
```
fatal error: sync: RUnlock of unlocked RWMutex
```
The RLock loop increases to `rw.readerCount = 1<<30`, after which `rw.Lock()` subtracts to `rw.readerCount = 0`, and then finally `rw.RUnlock()` errors because there appear to be zero readers.[^1]

[^1]: While this example has recursive read locking, one can add a goroutine to call RUnlock on behalf of each RLock without being blocked by the main goroutine, which results in logically non-reentrant behavior; example skips that for simplicity.

### Increase the limit?
That constant `1<<30` is `rwmutexMaxReaders` in [rwmutex.go](https://github.com/golang/go/blob/f22d37d574836ed4f1b42e283dda55dbd4d80aca/src/sync/rwmutex.go#L47). The name is a bit misleading because the actual max number of reader is `rwmutexMaxReaders-1`. More importantly, `rwmutexMaxReaders` can be safely increased to  `(1<<31)-1` with no other code changes. With a few other changes to account for representable `int32` constants, the strict upper bound `1<<31` is also possible. Would it be a worthwhile improvement for the implementation to have the tight bound `1<<31`, rather than an arbitrary limit?

issue.title: os: NewFile blocks if handle is blocked

labels.name: NeedsFix
labels.description: The path to resolution is known, but the work has not been done.

labels.name: Critical
labels.description: A critical problem that affects the availability or correctness of production systems built using Go

labels.name: BugReport
labels.description: Issues describing a possible bug in the Go implementation.

issue.body:
### Go version

go version go1.25.1 windows/arm64

### Output of `go env` in your module/workspace:

```shell
set AR=ar
set CC=gcc
set CGO_CFLAGS=-O2 -g
set CGO_CPPFLAGS=
set CGO_CXXFLAGS=-O2 -g
set CGO_ENABLED=1
set CGO_FFLAGS=-O2 -g
set CGO_LDFLAGS=-O2 -g
set CXX=g++
set GCCGO=gccgo
set GO111MODULE=
set GOARCH=arm64
set GOARM64=v8.0
set GOAUTH=netrc
set GOBIN=
set GOCACHE=C:\Users\qmuntaldiaz\AppData\Local\go-build
set GOCACHEPROG=
set GODEBUG=
set GOENV=C:\Users\qmuntaldiaz\AppData\Roaming\go\env
set GOEXE=.exe
set GOEXPERIMENT=
set GOFIPS140=off
set GOFLAGS=
set GOGCCFLAGS=-mthreads -fno-caret-diagnostics -Qunused-arguments -Wl,--no-gc-sections -fmessage-length=0 -ffile-prefix-map=C:\Users\QMUNTA~1\AppData\Local\Temp\go-build1302331125=/tmp/go-build -gno-record-gcc-switches
set GOHOSTARCH=arm64
set GOHOSTOS=windows
set GOINSECURE=
set GOMOD=NUL
set GOMODCACHE=C:\Users\qmuntaldiaz\go\pkg\mod
set GONOPROXY=github.com/microsoft
set GONOSUMDB=github.com/microsoft
set GOOS=windows
set GOPATH=C:\Users\qmuntaldiaz\go
set GOPRIVATE=github.com/microsoft
set GOPROXY=https://proxy.golang.org,direct
set GOROOT=C:\Users\qmuntaldiaz\sdk\go1.25.1
set GOSUMDB=sum.golang.org
set GOTELEMETRY=on
set GOTELEMETRYDIR=C:\Users\qmuntaldiaz\AppData\Roaming\go\telemetry
set GOTMPDIR=
set GOTOOLCHAIN=auto
set GOTOOLDIR=C:\Users\qmuntaldiaz\sdk\go1.25.1\pkg\tool\windows_arm64
set GOVCS=
set GOVERSION=go1.25.1
set GOWORK=
set PKG_CONFIG=pkg-config
```

### What did you do?

Call `os.NewFile` using a handle that is blocked doing I/O, like in here:
```go
package main

import (
	"fmt"
	"os"
	"syscall"
	"time"

	"golang.org/x/sys/windows"
)

func main() {
	const name = `\\.\pipe\go_testpipe`
	h, err := windows.CreateNamedPipe(syscall.StringToUTF16Ptr(name), windows.PIPE_ACCESS_DUPLEX, 0, 1, 4096, 4096, 0, nil)
	if err != nil {
		panic(err)
	}
	pipe := os.NewFile(uintptr(h), name) // OK
	defer pipe.Close()

	file, err := os.Open(name)
	if err != nil {
		panic(err)
	}
	defer file.Close()
	go func() {
		// Give some time for the Read to start and block.
		time.Sleep(100 * time.Millisecond)
		fmt.Println("NewFile()")
		os.NewFile(uintptr(file.Fd()), name) // Unexpected block
		fmt.Println("NewFile() returned")
	}()

	fmt.Println("Read()")
	var tmp [1]byte
	// Read will blocks
	file.Read(tmp[:]) // Expected block
	fmt.Println("Read() returned")
}
```

### What did you see happen?

`os.NewFile` blocks until the handle is unblocked:

```
Read()
NewFile()
```

### What did you expect to see?

`os.NewFile` doesn't block even if the handle is blocked:

```
Read()
NewFile()
NewFile() returned"
```

Note that this has always been the behavior until Go 1.25. The regression was introduced in [CL 662236](https://go-review.googlesource.com/c/go/+/662236).

This situation is more common than one would expect because the `os` package calls `NewFile(uintptr(syscall.Stdin), "/dev/stdin")` when initializing. And if someone has passed a blocked stdin handle, then the `os` package initialization hangs. This means that a Go application can hand even before calling any user-controlled code. See for example #75949.

@golang/windows

issue.body:
Hi Golang Team, 

As of now, c-shared build mode is not supported in AIX. We would like to understand the feasibility and technicalities involved to enable c-shared build mode in AIX. Any insights from the golang team about this will be of great help.

issue.body:
Hi Golang Team, 

As of now, c-shared build mode is not supported in AIX. We would like to understand the feasibility and technicalities involved to enable c-shared build mode in AIX. Any insights from the golang team about this will be of great help.

comment.body: CC @golang/compiler

issue.title: simd/archsimd: tests SIGILL on unsupported cpus, missing feature check

labels.name: NeedsInvestigation
labels.description: Someone must examine and confirm this is a valid issue and not a duplicate of an existing one.

labels.name: BugReport
labels.description: Issues describing a possible bug in the Go implementation.

issue.body:
I'm building Go1.26rc1 from source (all.bash)

The testing phase fails with the SIMD test.

```
##### GOEXPERIMENT=simd go test simd/archsimd/...
ok  	simd/archsimd	0.096s
SIGILL: illegal instruction
PC=0x548760 m=2 sigcode=2
instruction bytes: 0xc5 0xfd 0xfd 0xc1 0xc3 0xcc 0xcc 0xcc 0xcc 0xcc 0xcc 0xcc 0xcc 0xcc 0xcc 0xcc
```

I have an older amd64 CPU (i3-3225) which has limited SIMD instructions.


With regards to testing, all tests following this failure are "skipped due to earlier error"

Should GOEXPERIMENT tests be an automatic part of the test suite? (I hadn't specified any GOEXPERIMENTs)

comment.body:
I don't think this is a release blocker since the tests are mostly for the Go team itself, but this should probably be fixed before release.

I guess there's a missing feature flag check, or the CPU you're running falls in some gap that the current feature flags miss. The `SIGILL` is pretty clear.

issue.title: simd/archsimd: tests SIGILL on unsupported cpus, missing feature check

labels.name: NeedsInvestigation
labels.description: Someone must examine and confirm this is a valid issue and not a duplicate of an existing one.

labels.name: BugReport
labels.description: Issues describing a possible bug in the Go implementation.

I'm building Go1.26rc1 from source (all.bash)

issue.title: simd/archsimd: tests SIGILL on unsupported cpus, missing feature check

labels.name: NeedsInvestigation
labels.description: Someone must examine and confirm this is a valid issue and not a duplicate of an existing one.

labels.name: BugReport
labels.description: Issues describing a possible bug in the Go implementation.

issue.body:
I'm building Go1.26rc1 from source (all.bash)

The testing phase fails with the SIMD test.

```
##### GOEXPERIMENT=simd go test simd/archsimd/...
ok  	simd/archsimd	0.096s
SIGILL: illegal instruction
PC=0x548760 m=2 sigcode=2
instruction bytes: 0xc5 0xfd 0xfd 0xc1 0xc3 0xcc 0xcc 0xcc 0xcc 0xcc 0xcc 0xcc 0xcc 0xcc 0xcc 0xcc
```

I have an older amd64 CPU (i3-3225) which has limited SIMD instructions.


With regards to testing, all tests following this failure are "skipped due to earlier error"

Should GOEXPERIMENT tests be an automatic part of the test suite? (I hadn't specified any GOEXPERIMENTs)

issue.title: simd/archsimd: tests SIGILL on unsupported cpus, missing feature check

labels.name: NeedsInvestigation
labels.description: Someone must examine and confirm this is a valid issue and not a duplicate of an existing one.

labels.name: BugReport
labels.description: Issues describing a possible bug in the Go implementation.

issue.body:
I'm building Go1.26rc1 from source (all.bash)

The testing phase fails with the SIMD test.

```
##### GOEXPERIMENT=simd go test simd/archsimd/...
ok  	simd/archsimd	0.096s
SIGILL: illegal instruction
PC=0x548760 m=2 sigcode=2
instruction bytes: 0xc5 0xfd 0xfd 0xc1 0xc3 0xcc 0xcc 0xcc 0xcc 0xcc 0xcc 0xcc 0xcc 0xcc 0xcc 0xcc
```

I have an older amd64 CPU (i3-3225) which has limited SIMD instructions.


With regards to testing, all tests following this failure are "skipped due to earlier error"

Should GOEXPERIMENT tests be an automatic part of the test suite? (I hadn't specified any GOEXPERIMENTs)
